title: A block of mass $M$ is pulled along a horizontal frictionless surface by a rope of mass $m$. A force $F$ is applied at one end of the rope. The force exerted by the rope on the block is:
notes: Find the total acceleration of the system, then calculate the force on mass $M$.
solution_text: Total mass of the system $= M + m$. Acceleration $a = \frac{F}{M+m}$. The force exerted by the rope on the block is what accelerates block $M$. Thus, force $T = M a = \frac{F M}{M+m}$. So option B is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming the tension is $F$ throughout the rope, which is only true for a massless rope.

title: A block $A$ is connected to block $B$ by a string passing over a smooth pulley. If block $A$ slides down a vertical wall with velocity $v_A$, and the string makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical, the velocity of block $B$ moving horizontally is:
notes: The velocity component along the length of the string must be the same for both blocks.
solution_text: The string is inextensible, so the velocity component of block A along the string must equal the velocity of B. If the velocity of B is $v_B$, and the string is horizontal at B, then $v_B = v_A \cos\theta$. Hence option A is correct.
common_mistake: Resolving $v_A$ along the string incorrectly, or assuming $v_B = v_A$.

title: A block of mass $2\,\text{kg}$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface with coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.5$. If a horizontal force of $6\,\text{N}$ is applied to the block, the friction force acting on the block is ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$):
notes: Check if the applied force exceeds the maximum static friction ($f_{\text{max}} = \mu N$).
solution_text: Normal reaction $N = mg = 2 \times 10 = 20\,\text{N}$. Maximum static friction $f_{\text{max}} = \mu N = 0.5 \times 20 = 10\,\text{N}$. Since the applied force ($6\,\text{N}$) is less than $f_{\text{max}}$, the block remains at rest. The static friction force exactly balances the applied force. Thus, the friction force is $6\,\text{N}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Reporting $10\,\text{N}$ (the maximum possible static friction) directly without checking if the block actually moves.

title: A block of mass $m$ is placed on an inclined plane of angle $\theta$. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s$. The block starts sliding only when the inclination angle $\theta$ is greater than the angle of repose. The relationship for the angle of repose is:
notes: At the verge of sliding down the incline, the component of gravity down the incline balances the maximum static friction.
solution_text: Force down the incline: $mg \sin\theta$. Normal reaction: $N = mg \cos\theta$. Friction: $f = \mu_s N = \mu_s mg \cos\theta$. At the verge of sliding: $mg \sin\theta = \mu_s mg \cos\theta \implies \tan\theta = \mu_s \implies \theta = \tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$.
common_mistake: Confusing sine and tangent for the angle of repose.

title: A car is negotiating a curved road of radius $R$. If the coefficient of friction is $\mu$ and the road is flat, the maximum safe speed of the car is:
notes: The centripetal force is provided entirely by static friction: $\frac{mv^2}{R} \le \mu mg$.
solution_text: For a flat road, normal force $N = mg$. Centripetal force $F_c = mv^2/R$. Friction $f \le \mu mg$. Since friction provides the centripetal force: $mv^2/R \le \mu mg \implies v \le \sqrt{\mu Rg}$. The maximum safe speed is $\sqrt{\mu R g}$.
common_mistake: Thinking normal force has banking components on a flat road.

title: A bob of mass $m$ is suspended by a light string from the ceiling of a cart. If the cart accelerates horizontally with constant acceleration $a$, the angle $\theta$ made by the string with the vertical in equilibrium is:
notes: In the cart's frame, a pseudo force $ma$ acts horizontally opposite to the acceleration.
solution_text: In the accelerating cart's frame, two forces act on the bob in addition to tension $T$: downward gravity $mg$ and horizontal pseudo force $ma$ (opposite to cart's acceleration). In equilibrium: $T \sin\theta = ma$ and $T \cos\theta = mg$. Dividing these gives: $\tan\theta = a/g \implies \theta = \tan^{-1}(a/g)$.
common_mistake: Writing $\sin\theta = a/g$ by incorrectly forming the force triangle.

title: Two blocks of masses $m_1 = 4\,\text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 6\,\text{kg}$ are connected by a light string passing over a smooth massless pulley. The acceleration of the system is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
solution_text: Using the formula for Atwood machine: $a = \frac{(m_2 - m_1)g}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(6 - 4) \times 10}{6 + 4} = \frac{2 \times 10}{10} = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$.
common_mistake: Adding the forces in the numerator or subtracting the masses incorrectly.

title: A mass of $10\,\text{kg}$ is suspended vertically by two strings making angles of $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$ with the horizontal. The tension in the string making an angle of $30^\circ$ with the horizontal is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
solution_text: Let $T_1$ be the tension in the $30^\circ$ string and $T_2$ be the tension in the $60^\circ$ string. Horizontal equilibrium: $T_1 \cos 30^\circ = T_2 \cos 60^\circ \implies T_1 \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = T_2 \frac{1}{2} \implies T_2 = T_1 \sqrt{3}$. Vertical equilibrium: $T_1 \sin 30^\circ + T_2 \sin 60^\circ = mg \implies T_1 (1/2) + (T_1 \sqrt{3}) (\sqrt{3}/2) = 10 \times 10 \implies T_1/2 + 3T_1/2 = 100 \implies 2T_1 = 100 \implies T_1 = 50\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Mixing up the angles with the vertical instead of the horizontal.

title: A block of mass $5\,\text{kg}$ is pushed against a vertical wall with a horizontal force $F$. If the coefficient of static friction between the block and the wall is $\mu = 0.5$, the minimum force $F$ required to prevent the block from sliding down is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
solution_text: Normal force $N = F$. Vertical downward force is gravity $mg = 5 \times 10 = 50\,\text{N}$. Frictional force $f = \mu N = 0.5 F$. To prevent sliding: $f \ge mg \implies 0.5 F \ge 50 \implies F \ge 100\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Assuming normal force is $mg$, or multiplying $F$ by $g$ in the friction term.

title: A machine gun fires $360$ bullets per minute, each of mass $20\,\text{g}$ with a velocity of $500\,\text{m/s}$. The average force required to keep the gun in position is ________ $\text{N}$.
solution_text: Number of bullets per second: $n = 360 / 60 = 6$ bullets/s. Mass of bullet: $m = 20\,\text{g} = 0.02\,\text{kg}$. Velocity: $v = 500\,\text{m/s}$. Average force $F = n \cdot m \cdot v = 6 \times 0.02 \times 500 = 6 \times 10 = 60\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Using $360$ directly as bullets per second, or failing to convert mass of bullets from grams to kilograms.

title: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth wedge of inclination $\theta$ which in turn is placed on a horizontal surface. The acceleration $a$ that must be given to the wedge so that the block remains stationary relative to the wedge is:
notes: In the accelerating wedge's frame, a pseudo force $ma$ acts horizontally. Balance forces along the incline.
solution_text: In the wedge frame, the block experiences gravity $mg$ down, normal $N$ perpendicular to incline, and pseudo force $ma$ horizontally (opposite to acceleration). Along the inclined surface: force pushing down is $mg \sin\theta$; force pushing up is $ma \cos\theta$. For the block to remain stationary: $ma \cos\theta = mg \sin\theta \implies a = g \tan\theta$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming the acceleration must be $g \sin\theta$, which is the acceleration of the block sliding down the wedge when the wedge is stationary.

title: Three blocks of masses $1\,\text{kg}$, $2\,\text{kg}$ and $3\,\text{kg}$ are placed in contact on a frictionless horizontal table. A force of $12\,\text{N}$ is applied to the $1\,\text{kg}$ block. The acceleration of the system is ___ $\text{m/s}^2$ and the contact force between the $2\,\text{kg}$ and $3\,\text{kg}$ blocks is ___ $\text{N}$.
notes: Total acceleration $a = F / \sum m$. The contact force on the $3\,\text{kg}$ block accelerates only that block.
solution_text: Total mass $M = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6\,\text{kg}$. Acceleration of system $a = F/M = 12/6 = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$. The contact force $F_{23}$ between the $2\,\text{kg}$ and $3\,\text{kg}$ blocks is the only horizontal force accelerating the $3\,\text{kg}$ block. Thus, $F_{23} = m_3 a = 3 \times 2 = 6\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Calculating contact force between 1 and 2 blocks instead of 2 and 3 blocks, or subtracting contact forces incorrectly.

title: A spring balance is attached to the ceiling of a lift. When the lift is at rest, the balance reads $49\,\text{N}$. If the lift accelerates downward at $5\,\text{m/s}^2$, the reading of the spring balance is ($g = 9.8\,\text{m/s}^2$):
notes: Apparent weight in a downward accelerating lift is $m(g-a)$.
solution_text: Mass $m = 49/g = 49 / 9.8 = 5\,\text{kg}$. When accelerating downward: Apparent weight $= m(g-a) = 5(9.8 - 5) = 5 \times 4.8 = 24\,\text{N}$. Thus, the reading is $24\,\text{N}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Adding the acceleration instead of subtracting, yielding 74 N.

title: A block of mass $m$ is pushed up an inclined plane of angle $\theta$ with constant velocity by a horizontal force $F$. If the plane is smooth, the magnitude of $F$ is:
notes: Resolve forces along the inclined plane. The component of $F$ up the incline must balance the component of gravity down the incline.
solution_text: Component of horizontal force F along the incline is $F \cos\theta$. Component of gravity along the incline is $mg \sin\theta$. For constant velocity (no acceleration): $F \cos\theta = mg \sin\theta \implies F = mg \tan\theta$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming $F = mg \sin\theta$, which is only true for a force applied parallel to the incline.

title: Two blocks of masses $m$ and $2m$ are placed in contact on a frictionless horizontal table. A force $F$ is applied to block $m$ pushing it towards $2m$. The contact force between the blocks is:
notes: Find the acceleration of the entire system first, then calculate the force on mass $2m$.
solution_text: Acceleration of the system is $a = \frac{F}{m + 2m} = \frac{F}{3m}$. The contact force accelerates the block of mass $2m$. Force $= 2m \times a = 2m \left(\frac{F}{3m}\right) = \frac{2F}{3}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking the contact force is $F$, forgetting that some force accelerates block $m$.

title: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a double inclined wedge of mass $M$ with inclination angles $\theta$ on both sides. Find the acceleration of the wedge so that the block does not slide. This acceleration is $g \tan\theta$. If $\theta = 45^\circ$, this acceleration is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Apply the wedge acceleration condition: $a = g \tan\theta$.
solution_text: To prevent relative motion, the horizontal acceleration of the wedge must be $a = g \tan\theta$. Since $\theta = 45^\circ$, $a = 10 \times \tan 45^\circ = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$.
common_mistake: Using $g \sin\theta$ instead of $g \tan\theta$.

title: In a pulley system, block $A$ is suspended from a movable pulley, and block $B$ is connected directly to the string. If block $B$ moves with acceleration $a$ downwards, the acceleration of block $A$ is:
notes: Use the virtual work method: $\sum T \cdot x = 0$ or string length constraint.
solution_text: The tension pulling block A has two segments supporting the pulley, so total upward force is $2T$. The tension pulling block B is $T$. By virtual work: $2T \cdot x_A = T \cdot x_B \implies 2x_A = x_B$. Differentiating twice: $2a_A = a_B \implies a_A = a_B/2 = a/2$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking B has half the acceleration of A because it is directly connected.

title: Two blocks connected by a string over a smooth pulley are placed on a double inclined plane of angles $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$. The masses are $m_1 = 5\,\text{kg}$ (on $30^\circ$ plane) and $m_2 = 10\,\text{kg}$ (on $60^\circ$ plane). The acceleration of the system is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$). (Round to one decimal place, e.g. 4.1)
notes: Net driving force $= m_2 g \sin 60^\circ - m_1 g \sin 30^\circ$. Acceleration $= F_{\text{net}} / (m_1 + m_2)$.
solution_text: Driving force for $m_2$ down incline: $10 \times 10 \times \sin 60^\circ = 100 \times 0.866 = 86.6\,\text{N}$. Restraining force for $m_1$ along incline: $5 \times 10 \times \sin 30^\circ = 50 \times 0.5 = 25\,\text{N}$. Net driving force $= 86.6 - 25 = 61.6\,\text{N}$. Total mass $= 10 + 5 = 15\,\text{kg}$. Acceleration $= 61.6 / 15 \approx 4.1\,\text{m/s}^2$.
common_mistake: Neglecting the sine components of gravity along the inclined planes.

title: A mass $m$ is suspended by a system of three movable pulleys. If the free end of the string is pulled down with acceleration $a$, the acceleration of the mass $m$ is:
notes: Each movable pulley halves the displacement/velocity/acceleration.
solution_text: With 3 movable pulleys, each pulley reduces the displacement by a factor of 2. The relation is $x_m = x_f / 2^3 = x_f / 8$. Therefore, $a_m = a / 8$. Option D is correct.
common_mistake: Adding the pulley reductions instead of multiplying them (thinking $a/6$ instead of $a/8$).

title: A block of mass $10\,\text{kg}$ is placed on a rough horizontal table with $\mu_s = 0.6$ and $\mu_k = 0.4$. If a horizontal force of $50\,\text{N}$ is applied to the block, the acceleration of the block is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Find the maximum static friction $f_s = \mu_s N$. If the applied force is less than $f_s$, the block does not move.
solution_text: Normal force $N = mg = 10 \times 10 = 100\,\text{N}$. Maximum static friction $f_{s,\text{max}} = \mu_s N = 0.6 \times 100 = 60\,\text{N}$. Since the applied force of $50\,\text{N}$ is less than the maximum static friction ($60\,\text{N}$), the block will not move. Therefore, its acceleration is 0.
common_mistake: Using kinetic friction directly to calculate acceleration: $(50 - 40)/10 = 1\,\text{m/s}^2$, without checking if the block starts moving.

title: A block of mass $m$ is placed on an incline of angle $\theta$. The minimum force parallel to the incline required to push it up is $F_1$, and to prevent it from sliding down is $F_2$. If $\theta = 45^\circ$ and $\mu = 0.5$, the ratio $F_1 / F_2$ is:
notes: Write equations: $F_1 = mg \sin\theta + \mu mg \cos\theta$ (friction opposes motion up), and $F_2 = mg \sin\theta - \mu mg \cos\theta$ (friction assists preventing slide down).
solution_text: $F_1 = mg(\sin\theta + \mu \cos\theta)$ and $F_2 = mg(\sin\theta - \mu \cos\theta)$. For $\theta = 45^\circ$, $\sin\theta = \cos\theta = 1/\sqrt{2}$. Ratio $F_1 / F_2 = \frac{1 + \mu}{1 - \mu} = \frac{1 + 0.5}{1 - 0.5} = 1.5 / 0.5 = 3$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Adding friction in both equations or subtracting in both.

title: Two blocks of masses $m_1 = 2\,\text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 3\,\text{kg}$ are stacked on top of each other. The coefficient of static friction between them is $0.2$, and the table is smooth. The maximum horizontal force $F$ applied to the bottom block ($m_2$) so that they move together is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Find maximum acceleration of top block: $a_{\text{max}} = \mu g$. Then apply it to the total system: $F_{\text{max}} = (m_1 + m_2) a_{\text{max}}$.
solution_text: The top block of mass $m_1 = 2\,\text{kg}$ is accelerated only by friction. The maximum friction is $f_{\text{max}} = \mu m_1 g = 0.2 \times 2 \times 10 = 4\,\text{N}$. The maximum acceleration of top block is $a_{\text{max}} = f_{\text{max}} / m_1 = 4 / 2 = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$. For both to move together, the maximum acceleration of the system must be $2\,\text{m/s}^2$. Total mass $= 2 + 3 = 5\,\text{kg}$. Maximum force $F = (m_1 + m_2) a_{\text{max}} = 5 \times 2 = 10\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Applying force to top block instead of bottom, or using wrong mass for friction calculation.

title: The angle of banking $\theta$ of a road of radius $R$ for safe speed $v$ without depending on friction is given by $\theta = \tan^{-1}(v^2 / Rg)$. If a road of radius $90\,\text{m}$ is designed for a speed of $30\,\text{m/s}$, the angle of banking is:
notes: Apply $\tan\theta = v^2/Rg$.
solution_text: $\tan\theta = v^2 / Rg = 30^2 / (90 \times 10) = 900 / 900 = 1$. Since $\tan\theta = 1$, the angle of banking is $\theta = 45^\circ$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using $v$ instead of $v^2$ in the formula, leading to wrong angle.

title: A cyclist speeds around a circular track of radius $80\,\text{m}$ with a speed of $20\,\text{m/s}$. The angle of inclination with the vertical to maintain balance is $\tan^{-1}(1/x)$ where $x$ is ________.
notes: Angle with vertical: $\tan\theta = v^2 / Rg$.
solution_text: Angle with vertical is given by $\tan\theta = v^2 / Rg$. Here $v = 20\,\text{m/s}$, $R = 80\,\text{m}$, $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$. $\tan\theta = 20^2 / (80 \times 10) = 400 / 800 = 1/2$. Thus, $x = 2$.
common_mistake: Confusing angle with vertical vs angle with horizontal (which is $\cot\theta$).

title: A coin is placed at a distance $r$ from the center of a rotating turntable. If the coefficient of static friction is $\mu$, the maximum angular velocity $\omega$ for which the coin does not slip is:
notes: Friction provides the centripetal force: $m\omega^2 r \le \mu mg$.
solution_text: Centripetal force is $m\omega^2 r$. Frictional limit is $\mu mg$. For no slipping: $m\omega^2 r \le \mu mg \implies \omega^2 \le \mu g/r \implies \omega \le \sqrt{\mu g/r}$. The maximum angular velocity is $\sqrt{\mu g / r}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing the position of $r$ in the numerator vs denominator.

title: A stone of mass $0.5\,\text{kg}$ is whirled in a vertical circle of radius $1\,\text{m}$. The tension in the string at the lowest point when its speed is $5\,\text{m/s}$ is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: At the lowest point, the tension must balance gravity and provide centripetal force: $T = mg + mv^2/R$.
solution_text: At lowest point: $T - mg = mv^2/R \implies T = mg + mv^2/R$. Here $m = 0.5\,\text{kg}$, $v = 5\,\text{m/s}$, $R = 1\,\text{m}$, $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$. $T = 0.5 \times 10 + 0.5 \times 5^2 / 1 = 5 + 12.5 = 17.5\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Subtracting $mg$ instead of adding it, yielding 7.5 N.

title: A conical pendulum has a string of length $L$ and bob of mass $m$. The string makes a constant angle $\theta$ with the vertical. The tension in the string is:
notes: Resolve forces: the vertical component of tension balances gravity: $T \cos\theta = mg$.
solution_text: For a conical pendulum in horizontal circular motion, the vertical forces must balance: $T \cos\theta = mg \implies T = mg / \cos\theta$. The horizontal component $T \sin\theta$ provides the centripetal force $m\omega^2 r$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking $T = mg \cos\theta$, which is the tension in a regular pendulum when it is at an angle $\theta$ and has zero speed.

title: A pendulum bob of mass $m$ hangs from the ceiling of an elevator. If the elevator decelerates upwards at $2\,\text{m/s}^2$, the effective acceleration due to gravity is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Deceleration upwards is equivalent to an acceleration downwards. The pseudo force is upwards: $g_{\text{eff}} = g - a$.
solution_text: An upward deceleration is acceleration in the downward direction ($a = -2\,\text{m/s}^2$ upwards). The pseudo force on bob is upward: $F_p = ma$ upwards. Effective gravity $g_{\text{eff}} = g - a = 10 - 2 = 8\,\text{m/s}^2$.
common_mistake: Adding deceleration to $g$ because it is 'upward', yielding 12 m/s².

title: A beaker filled with water is accelerated horizontally with constant acceleration $a$. The angle $\theta$ that the free surface of water makes with the horizontal is:
notes: The surface of the liquid must align perpendicular to the effective gravity vector.
solution_text: The effective gravity vector has components $g$ downward and $a$ horizontal (via pseudo force in liquid frame). The angle of the liquid surface with horizontal is given by $\tan\theta = a_{\text{horizontal}} / a_{\text{vertical}} = a/g \implies \theta = \tan^{-1}(a/g)$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Writing $\theta = \tan^{-1}(g/a)$, mixing up the horizontal and vertical components.

title: A block of mass $1\,\text{kg}$ is placed on the rough horizontal floor of a cart. The coefficient of friction is $0.4$. If the cart accelerates horizontally at $3\,\text{m/s}^2$, the friction force acting on the block is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: First find the maximum static friction force. Compare the required force (pseudo force) to see if it slips.
solution_text: In the cart frame, the block experiences a pseudo force $F_p = ma = 1 \times 3 = 3\,\text{N}$. The maximum static friction is $f_{\text{max}} = \mu mg = 0.4 \times 1 \times 10 = 4\,\text{N}$. Since the pseudo force ($3\,\text{N}$) is less than $f_{\text{max}}$ ($4\,\text{N}$), the block does not slip. Frictional force equals the applied force $= 3\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Using $4\,\text{N}$ directly (limit friction) without checking if the pseudo force exceeds it.

title: A rocket of mass $1000\,\text{kg}$ ejects fuel at a rate of $10\,\text{kg/s}$ with a velocity of $2000\,\text{m/s}$ relative to the rocket. The upthrust force on the rocket is ________ $\text{N}$.
notes: Upthrust on a rocket is given by $F = v_{\text{rel}} \frac{dm}{dt}$.
solution_text: Upthrust force is $F = v_{\text{rel}} \frac{dm}{dt}$. Given $v_{\text{rel}} = 2000\,\text{m/s}$ and $\frac{dm}{dt} = 10\,\text{kg/s}$. $F = 2000 \times 10 = 20000\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Subtracting rocket weight or dividing by rocket mass, confusing thrust force with net acceleration.

title: A stream of water of cross-sectional area $A$ and density $\rho$ strikes a vertical wall normally with velocity $v$ and splashes off sideways. The force exerted on the wall is:
notes: Apply impulse-momentum theorem: Force is the rate of momentum change of water hitting the wall.
solution_text: Mass of water striking the wall per second: $\frac{dm}{dt} = \rho A v$. Since it splashes off sideways, its final velocity perpendicular to the wall is 0. Change in momentum per second: $F = v \frac{dm}{dt} = v (\rho A v) = \rho A v^2$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $2\rho A v^2$ by assuming the water rebounds, but the problem states it splashes off sideways (final velocity 0).

title: A hammer of mass $1\,\text{kg}$ moving at $6\,\text{m/s}$ strikes a nail and comes to rest in $0.01\,\text{s}$. The average force exerted on the nail is ________ $\text{N}$.
notes: Average force is change in momentum divided by time: $F_{\text{avg}} = \Delta P / \Delta t$.
solution_text: Change in momentum $\Delta P = m(v_f - v_i) = 1(0 - 6) = -6\,\text{kg\,m/s}$. Average force magnitude $= |\Delta P| / \Delta t = 6 / 0.01 = 600\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Multiplying by $0.01$ instead of dividing, yielding 0.06 N.

title: Sand drops vertically at a rate of $2\,\text{kg/s}$ onto a conveyor belt moving horizontally at a constant speed of $3\,\text{m/s}$. The extra power required to keep the belt moving is ________ $\text{W}$.
notes: Extra force is $F = v \frac{dm}{dt}$. Power is $P = F v = v^2 \frac{dm}{dt}$.
solution_text: The force to accelerate the falling sand to the speed of the belt is $F = v \frac{dm}{dt} = 3 \times 2 = 6\,\text{N}$. Power delivered by the belt's motor is $P = F v = 6 \times 3 = 18\,\text{W}$. Note: half of this power is dissipated as heat.
common_mistake: Calculating power as kinetic energy change per second: $\frac{1}{2} \frac{dm}{dt} v^2 = 9\,\text{W}$, forgetting that the collision is completely inelastic and requires double the energy.

title: A block of mass $5\,\text{kg}$ is suspended by a light string. A horizontal force $F$ is applied to the block such that the string makes an angle of $30^\circ$ with the vertical in equilibrium. The magnitude of the force $F$ is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$). (Round to nearest integer, use $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$)
notes: In equilibrium, the horizontal forces balance: $T \sin\theta = F$. The vertical forces balance: $T \cos\theta = mg$. Dividing the two equations: $F = mg \tan\theta$.
solution_text:
In equilibrium, the horizontal forces balance: $T \sin\theta = F$.
The vertical forces balance: $T \cos\theta = mg$.
Dividing the two equations: $F = mg \tan\theta$.
Here, $m = 5\,\text{kg}$, $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$, and $\theta = 30^\circ$.
$F = 50 \times \tan 30^\circ = \frac{50}{\sqrt{3}} \approx \frac{50}{1.732} \approx 28.87\,\text{N} \approx 29\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Using $\sin 30^\circ$ instead of $\tan 30^\circ$ by forming the wrong force triangle.

title: Two blocks of masses $4\,\text{kg}$ and $6\,\text{kg}$ are placed in contact on a smooth horizontal surface. A horizontal force of $20\,\text{N}$ is applied to the $4\,\text{kg}$ block. The acceleration of the blocks is ___ $\text{m/s}^2$ and the contact force between them is ___ $\text{N}$.
notes: Find the total acceleration first, then apply Newton's second law on the $6\,\text{kg}$ block.
solution_text:
The total acceleration of the system is $a = \frac{F}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{20}{4+6} = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$.
The contact force $N$ between the blocks is the only horizontal force accelerating the $6\,\text{kg}$ block.
Thus, $N = m_2 a = 6 \times 2 = 12\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Assuming the contact force is $20\,\text{N}$, forgetting that some force accelerates the $4\,\text{kg}$ block.

title: Two blocks of masses $m_1 = 1\,\text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 1\,\text{kg}$ are connected by a string and hang on one side of a smooth pulley, while a block $m_3 = 6\,\text{kg}$ hangs on the other side. The tension in the string connecting $m_1$ and $m_2$ (where $m_1$ is the bottom-most block hanging below $m_2$) is ________ $\text{N}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Find acceleration of the entire system first, then draw FBD for block $m_1$.
solution_text:
The total mass on the left side is $m_{\text{left}} = m_1 + m_2 = 1 + 1 = 2\,\text{kg}$.
The mass on the right side is $m_3 = 6\,\text{kg}$.
Since $m_3 > m_{\text{left}}$, the system accelerates: $a = \frac{(m_3 - m_{\text{left}})g}{m_3 + m_{\text{left}}} = \frac{(6 - 2) \times 10}{6 + 2} = \frac{40}{8} = 5\,\text{m/s}^2$.
Now, consider the bottom-most block $m_1 = 1\,\text{kg}$ which accelerates upwards at $5\,\text{m/s}^2$:
$T_1 - m_1 g = m_1 a \implies T_1 = m_1(g + a) = 1 \times (10 + 5) = 15\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Using $T_1 = m_1 g = 10\,\text{N}$, neglecting the acceleration of the system.

title: A block $A$ is moving horizontally to the left with speed $v$. A string attached to $A$ passes over a fixed pulley and connects to block $B$. If the string makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal at block $A$, the velocity of block $B$ moving upwards is:
notes: The velocity along the string must be equal at both ends of the inextensible string.
solution_text:
The velocity of block A along the line of the string is $v \cos\theta$.
Since the string is inextensible, this must equal the velocity of block B.
Hence, $v_B = v \cos\theta$.
Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Dividing by $\cos\theta$ instead of multiplying, which is a common confusion in constraint velocity problems.

title: Two blocks of masses $m_1 = 3\,\text{kg}$ and $m_2 = 1\,\text{kg}$ are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth fixed pulley. The acceleration of the center of mass of the two blocks is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: First, find the acceleration of the blocks, then apply the center of mass acceleration formula.
solution_text:
Acceleration of individual blocks is $a = \frac{(m_1 - m_2)g}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{(3 - 1) \times 10}{3 + 1} = 5\,\text{m/s}^2$.
Taking downward as negative and upward as positive:
$a_1 = -5\,\text{m/s}^2$ (for $3\,\text{kg}$ block), $a_2 = +5\,\text{m/s}^2$ (for $1\,\text{kg}$ block).
The acceleration of center of mass is:
$a_{\text{cm}} = \frac{m_1 a_1 + m_2 a_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{3(-5) + 1(5)}{3 + 1} = \frac{-15 + 5}{4} = -2.5\,\text{m/s}^2$.
The magnitude is $2.5\,\text{m/s}^2$.
common_mistake: Adding the acceleration values without taking their opposing directions into account.

title: A block of mass $10\,\text{kg}$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.5$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction is $\mu_k = 0.4$. A horizontal force of $45\,\text{N}$ is applied to the block. The acceleration of the block is ________ $\text{m/s}^2$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Verify if the applied force exceeds the maximum static friction limit $f_{\text{max}} = \mu_s N$.
solution_text:
Normal reaction $N = mg = 10 \times 10 = 100\,\text{N}$.
Maximum static friction $f_{\text{max}} = \mu_s N = 0.5 \times 100 = 50\,\text{N}$.
Since the applied force of $45\,\text{N}$ is less than the maximum static friction force ($50\,\text{N}$), the block remains at rest.
Therefore, its acceleration is 0.
common_mistake: Calculating acceleration using kinetic friction: $a = \frac{45 - 40}{10} = 0.5\,\text{m/s}^2$, without verifying if the block moves.

title: A block of mass $m$ is pressed against a vertical wall by a horizontal force $F$. If the coefficient of static friction between the block and the wall is $\mu_s$, the minimum force $F$ required to keep the block stationary is:
notes: For the vertical wall configuration, normal force equals $F$, and the upward static friction must balance $mg$.
solution_text:
For the block to remain stationary, the upward friction force $f$ must balance the downward gravity $mg$: $f = mg$.
Static friction limit: $f \le \mu_s N$.
Here, the normal force is $N = F$.
So, $mg \le \mu_s F \implies F \ge \frac{mg}{\mu_s}$.
The minimum force required is $mg / \mu_s$.
Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Selecting $\mu_s mg$, confusing the vertical wall layout with a horizontal table layout.

title: A turn of radius $60\,\text{m}$ is banked at an angle of $45^\circ$. If the coefficient of static friction between the tires of a car and the road is $0.2$, the maximum safe speed of the car is ________ $\text{m/s}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Use the banked turn maximum velocity formula: $v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{Rg \frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}}$.
solution_text:
The formula for the maximum safe speed on a banked road with friction is:
$v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{Rg \left(\frac{\tan\theta + \mu}{1 - \mu \tan\theta}\right)}$.
Substituting the given values ($R = 60\,\text{m}$, $\theta = 45^\circ \implies \tan\theta = 1$, $\mu = 0.2$, $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$):
$v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{60 \times 10 \times \left(\frac{1 + 0.2}{1 - 0.2}\right)} = \sqrt{600 \times \frac{1.2}{0.8}} = \sqrt{600 \times 1.5} = \sqrt{900} = 30\,\text{m/s}$.
common_mistake: Using flat road formula $\sqrt{\mu Rg}$ or neglecting friction entirely.

title: A bucket full of water is whirled in a vertical circle of radius $R$. The minimum speed at the top of the circle so that the water does not spill out is:
notes: At the top of the vertical circle, critical speed corresponds to normal force $N = 0$.
solution_text:
At the top of the vertical circle, the forces are gravity $mg$ and the normal force $N$ downwards.
$N + mg = \frac{mv^2}{R}$.
For the water not to spill, the normal force $N \ge 0$.
In the limiting case, $N = 0 \implies mg = \frac{mv^2}{R} \implies v = \sqrt{gR}$.
Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $\sqrt{5gR}$ which is the minimum velocity required at the lowest point of a vertical circle.

title: A lift is going up with an acceleration $a = 2\,\text{m/s}^2$. A man of mass $60\,\text{kg}$ standing in the lift releases a coin from his hand from a height of $2\,\text{m}$ above the floor of the lift. The time taken by the coin to reach the floor of the lift is ________ $\text{s}$. (Take $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$, round to two decimal places)
notes: Work in the lift's reference frame where the coin has an effective acceleration $g_{\text{eff}} = g + a$.
solution_text:
In the lift's accelerating frame, the coin experiences a downward pseudo force, so its effective acceleration is:
$g_{\text{eff}} = g + a = 10 + 2 = 12\,\text{m/s}^2$.
The coin falls a distance of $h = 2\,\text{m}$ from rest relative to the lift.
Using $h = \frac{1}{2} g_{\text{eff}} t^2$:
$2 = \frac{1}{2} (12) t^2 \implies 2 = 6 t^2 \implies t^2 = \frac{1}{3} \implies t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.58\,\text{s}$.
common_mistake: Using $g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$ instead of $g_{\text{eff}} = 12\,\text{m/s}^2$.

title: In a non-inertial frame of reference, Newton's second law is written as $\vec{F} - m\vec{a}_0 = m\vec{a}$, where $\vec{a}_0$ is the acceleration of the frame. The term $-m\vec{a}_0$ is called:
notes: Identify the additional term required to make Newton's second law valid in non-inertial frames.
solution_text:
The term $-m\vec{a}_0$ is the pseudo force (or inertial force) which corrects for the acceleration of the frame.
Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Calling it centripetal force, which is a type of real force.

title: A block of mass $m$ is placed on a smooth inclined plane of angle $\theta$ which is fixed inside an elevator. If the elevator accelerates downwards with acceleration $a$ ($a < g$), the acceleration of the block relative to the incline is:
notes: Use the effective gravity $g_{\text{eff}} = g - a$ downwards in the elevator's frame.
solution_text:
In the downward accelerating elevator's frame, the effective gravity is $g_{\text{eff}} = g - a$.
The acceleration component of the block along the incline is $g_{\text{eff}} \sin\theta = (g - a) \sin\theta$.
Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Adding the acceleration instead of subtracting it.

title: A ball of mass $0.15\,\text{kg}$ hits a wall normally with a speed of $20\,\text{m/s}$ and bounces back with the same speed. The impulse imparted to the wall is ________ $\text{N\cdot s}$.
notes: Calculate momentum change $\Delta P = m(v_f - v_i)$ taking direction into account.
solution_text:
Taking the direction towards the wall as positive, initial velocity $v_i = 20\,\text{m/s}$, and final velocity $v_f = -20\,\text{m/s}$.
The change in momentum is: $\Delta P = m(v_f - v_i) = 0.15 \times (-20 - 20) = 0.15 \times (-40) = -6\,\text{kg\,m/s}$.
Magnitude of impulse imparted to the wall is $6\,\text{N\cdot s}$.
common_mistake: Thinking impulse is zero since initial and final speeds are equal, or calculating $0.15 \times 20 = 3\,\text{N\cdot s}$ by neglecting vector directions.

title: The variable mass equation for a rocket is $F_{\text{thrust}} = u_{\text{rel}} \left(-\frac{dm}{dt}\right)$, where $u_{\text{rel}}$ is the speed of exhaust gases relative to the rocket. The term $-\frac{dm}{dt}$ represents:

options.A: Rate of increase of rocket mass

notes: The negative derivative of mass reflects the positive rate at which fuel is burned and ejected.
solution_text:
Since fuel is being ejected, the rocket's mass decreases over time, so $dm/dt < 0$.
Therefore, $-dm/dt$ is a positive quantity representing the rate of consumption of fuel.
Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing it with the total mass of the rocket.

title: A cart of initial mass $M_0$ starts from rest and is pushed by a constant force $F$. Sand leaks out from the bottom of the cart at a constant rate $r$. The velocity of the cart as a function of time $t$ (before all sand runs out) is:
notes: Leaking sand has zero velocity relative to the cart, so the thrust force is zero. Solve $F = M(t) \frac{dv}{dt}$.
solution_text:
The mass at time $t$ is $M(t) = M_0 - rt$.
Since sand leaks out vertically, it has zero horizontal velocity relative to the cart. Hence, there is no thrust force from the leak.
The equation of motion is: $F = M(t) \frac{dv}{dt} = (M_0 - rt) \frac{dv}{dt}$.
Rearranging and integrating: $\int_0^v dv = \int_0^t \frac{F}{M_0 - rx} dx \implies v(t) = \frac{F}{r} \ln\left(\frac{M_0}{M_0 - rt}\right)$.
Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Incorrectly assuming $F = \frac{d}{dt}(Mv) = M \frac{dv}{dt} + v \frac{dM}{dt}$ which is only for systems capturing external mass.
